Unit Five: The Human Body Systems 🤸‍♀️

Unit 5 on New Curriculum

Think of your body as an incredibly advanced biological machine! It's made up of different teams, called organ systems, that all work together to keep you alive and kicking. These systems include everything from your breathing (respiratory) and eating (digestive) systems to your nervous system that acts like the body's internet. In this unit, we're going to zoom in on two amazing systems: the musculoskeletal system (how you move) and the reproductive system.

Unit learning competencies

After the successful completion of this unit, the student will be able to:

  • Explain the structure and function of the musculoskeletal system.
  • Identify the structures and functions of the male and female reproductive systems.
  • Explain ways to prevent or reduce the spread of STIS/ HIV.
  • Explain family planning strategies.
  • Examine the effect of alcohol use, chewing Khat, cannabis and other drug uses on the normal functioning of the musculoskeletal and reproductive systems.
  • Identify harmful practices that affect human reproductive health.

Human Musculoskeletal Systems 💪🦴

Ready to learn how you can walk, run, jump, and lift things? You can thank your musculoskeletal system for that! This is the amazing organ system that gives you the ability to move. It’s a team-up between your muscles and your skeleton. Together, they give your body its shape and form, protect your important internal organs (like your heart and brain), and allow you to interact with the world.

The human musculoskeletal system is built from several key parts:

  1. Muscles: These are the engines of your body! They pull on bones to make them move.
  2. Joints and Cartilages: Joints are where bones meet. Cartilage is the smooth, rubbery stuff that covers the ends of bones to stop them from grinding against each other. Ouch!
  3. Tendons: These are like super-strong ropes that connect your muscles to your bones.
  4. Ligaments: These are fibrous, stretchy bands that connect bone to bone, holding your skeleton together.
  5. Connective Tissues: This is the "body glue" that holds everything in place and helps everything move smoothly.

Types of Muscles

Did you know you have about 700 muscles in your body? They make up almost half of your body weight! 🤯 Muscles are made of special tissues that can contract (get shorter) to create movement. There are three main types of muscles in your body.

1. The Cardiac Muscles ❤️

This is the superstar muscle of your body—the heart! The cardiac muscle, also called the myocardium, forms the thick walls of your heart.

It's an involuntary muscle, which means you don't have to think about making it beat. It just works automatically, 24/7, to pump blood all around your body. Its cells are linked by special junctions called intercalated discs that help them all beat in perfect rhythm.

2. The Skeletal Muscles 💪

These are the muscles you probably think of when you hear the word "muscle." Skeletal muscles are attached to your bones by tendons. They are voluntary muscles, meaning you control them when you want to move. Want to kick a ball, wave to a friend, or lift your backpack? That's your skeletal muscles at work! They make up about 30% to 40% of your total body mass.

3. The Smooth Muscles 😮

Smooth muscles are the silent, automatic workers inside your body. They are found in the walls of hollow organs like your stomach, intestines, bladder, and blood vessels. Like cardiac muscle, they are involuntary. You don't have to tell your stomach to digest food or your blood vessels to squeeze—smooth muscles handle it all behind the scenes. They are called "smooth" because, unlike skeletal and cardiac muscles, they don't have stripes (striations).

Example 1: Muscle Types in Action

When you eat an apple, all three muscle types are working! 🍎

  • Your skeletal muscles (voluntary) move your jaw to chew and your arm to lift the apple.
  • Smooth muscles (involuntary) in your esophagus and stomach push the food along and start digesting it.
  • Your cardiac muscle (involuntary) keeps beating, pumping blood to provide the energy for all this activity.

Mechanism of actions of skeletal muscles

Ever wonder how a muscle actually works? It's like a tiny, biological rope-pulling contest! Inside each muscle fiber are two main protein filaments: a thick one called myosin and a thin one called actin. Muscle contraction is basically the myosin filaments pulling the actin filaments closer together.

The Sliding Filament Theory: A Simple Breakdown

Here’s how your muscles contract in a few simple steps:

  1. Activation Signal 🧠: Your brain sends an electrical signal down a nerve to the muscle. This signal tells the muscle to release a special ion: calcium.
  2. The Calcium Key 🔑: Calcium acts like a key. It unlocks the actin filaments, allowing the myosin heads to grab onto them.
  3. The Power Stroke 💪: Using energy from a molecule called ATP (the body's fuel), the myosin heads pull the actin filaments inward. This is the "contraction" that makes the muscle shorter and creates movement!
  4. Relaxation 😌: When the nerve signal stops, the calcium is pumped away. The actin filaments lock up again, the myosin lets go, and the muscle relaxes.

Types of Muscle Contractions

Not all contractions are the same! There are three main types:

  • Isometric Contraction: The muscle is tense and working, but it doesn't change length. Think about pushing against a wall—your muscles are firing, but nothing is moving.
  • Concentric Contraction: This is when the muscle shortens as it contracts. The classic example is the "up" phase of a bicep curl when you're lifting a weight.
  • Eccentric Contraction: The muscle lengthens while still under tension. This happens when you slowly lower the weight in a bicep curl. It acts as a brake.

The Human Axial and Appendicular Skeletons

Your skeleton is the amazing framework that holds you up! An adult has 206 bones, but you were born with around 300! As you grow, many of these smaller bones fuse together. Bones are made of a strong, lightweight mix of a protein called collagen and a mineral called calcium phosphate.

The Bone-Building Crew 👷‍♀️

Your bones are alive and are constantly being rebuilt by four types of special cells:

  • Osteoblasts: The "Builders." They create new bone tissue.
  • Osteocytes: The "Supervisors." These are mature bone cells that maintain the bone.
  • Osteoclasts: The "Demolition Crew." They break down old or damaged bone to make way for new bone.
  • Osteoprogenitor: The "Repair Crew." These stem cells are crucial for fixing broken bones.
Types of bones Names and parts
Long bones Femur and tibia (bones longer than wide)
Short bones Carpus (bone of wrists)
Flat bones Cranium (skull), ilium (pelvis), sternum, and ribs
Irregular bones Vertebrae, sacrum, coccyx, temporal, mandible, palatine, nasal, etc.
Sesamoid bones Small sesame seed-like bones that are embedded in the muscles

Functions of Bones

Your bones do more than just hold you up! They have five major jobs:

  1. Support and Movement: They provide the rigid structure for your muscles to pull against, allowing you to move.
  2. Protection: They form a protective cage around your most vital organs. Your skull protects your brain, and your ribs protect your heart and lungs.
  3. Producing Blood Cells: Deep inside some bones is a soft tissue called bone marrow, which is a factory for making new red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets.
  4. Storing Fat: Certain bone marrows store fat, which can be used as an energy reserve.
  5. Storing Minerals: Bones are like a bank for important minerals, especially calcium and phosphorus. If your body needs more, it can make a "withdrawal" from your bones.

1. The Axial Skeleton

The skeleton is divided into two main parts. The axial skeleton is the central core of your body. It includes the bones that form your head, neck, and trunk. Its main job is to protect your most important organs.

Parts of the axial skeleton Number of bones Functions
Skull (Cranium 8 + Facial 14) 22 It holds and protects the brain.
Ossicles (both ears, 3+3) 6 Auditory
Hyoid (neck) 1 Holds the head
Thoracic (rib cage) 24 It protects the heart and lungs.
Thorax (sternum) 1 Bind and handle the abdomen
Vertebral column 26 Holds up human body
Total bones 80

2. The Appendicular Skeleton

The appendicular skeleton includes all the bones of your "appendages"—that means your arms and legs! It also includes the bones that connect your limbs to the axial skeleton: the shoulder girdle and the pelvic girdle. This part of the skeleton is all about movement.

Appendicular skeleton parts Number of bones
Shoulder girdles 4
Pelvis 2
Arms and forearms 6
Thighs and legs 8
Hands 54
Feet and ankles 52
Sum 126

Total Bones Calculation 🧮

Let's put it all together! The total number of bones in the human body is the sum of the axial and appendicular skeletons.

80 (axial bones) + 126 (appendicular bones) = 206 bones in total!

Joints

So, how do all these bones move? At the joints! A joint is any place where two or more bones meet. Joints, along with their surrounding tissues, are what allow your body to bend, twist, and move in all sorts of ways.

Types of Joints

Joints come in several different types, each allowing for a specific kind of movement.

  • 1. The Ball and Socket Joint: Allows for the most freedom of movement. The rounded end of one bone (the ball) fits into a cup-like socket of another. You can move it in all directions. Examples: Shoulder and hip joints.
  • 2. Hinge Joints: These work just like a door hinge, allowing movement in only one direction (opening and closing). Examples: Elbow and knee joints.
  • 3. Condyloid Joints(Ellipsoid Joints): These allow movement back and forth and side to side, but no rotation. Examples: The joints in your fingers and jaw.
  • 4. Pivot Joints: These allow for a twisting or rotating motion, where one bone swivels in a ring formed by another. Example: The joint that lets you turn your head side to side.
  • 5. Gliding Joints: These joints allow bones to slide over one another, providing limited movement. Example: The joints in your wrist.
  • 6. Saddle Joint: This unique joint allows back-and-forth and side-to-side movement, but not rotation. It's what gives your thumb its ability to move and grasp things. Example: The base of your thumb.

The Reproductive System

The main job of the human reproductive system is to create and deliver sperm (from males) and eggs (from females). When a male and female have sexual intercourse, the male's penis is inserted into the female's vagina. Sperm is then ejaculated and begins an amazing journey through the cervix, into the uterus, and up the fallopian tubes to meet and fertilize an egg. This process is called internal fertilization, and it's the first step in creating a new human life! 🌟

Human Reproductive System (Male and Female)

The Male Reproductive System 🚶‍♂️

The male reproductive system has parts on the outside (external genitals like the penis, testes, and scrotum) and parts on the inside (like the prostate gland, vas deferens, and urethra). They all work together as a team to do three main things: produce sperm (male gametes), make male sex hormones, and create other parts of semen.

1. The Penis

The penis is the male organ for intercourse. It's made of soft, spongy tissue, muscles, blood vessels, and the urethra. It has two main jobs: a urinary function (peeing) and a sexual function. The sexual part happens in two stages: erection and ejaculation.

What is an erection? 🤔

An erection is when the penis becomes stiff and firm. This usually happens because of sexual arousal, but it can also happen during sleep. It's all about blood flow! Arteries that supply the penis widen, letting more blood rush into the spongy tissues (corpus cavernosa and corpus spongiosum). This blood gets "trapped," making the penis engorged and sustaining the erection.

2. Urethra

The urethra is a tube that connects the bladder to the outside world. In males, it's a dual-purpose tube! It carries urine out of the body (urination) and also carries sperm out during ejaculation.

3. The Scrotum

The scrotum is the sac of skin that holds and protects the testes. It's like a personal climate control system for the testes, keeping them at the perfect temperature to make healthy sperm. It also contains other important parts like seminal vesicles and the vas deferens.

4. Testes

The testes (or testicles) are two oval-shaped bodies found in the scrotum. They are the factories of the male reproductive system, with two crucial jobs:

  • Producing testosterone, the main male sex hormone.
  • Producing millions of sperm through a process called spermatogenesis.
Structure Function Explained Simply 🤩
Testes The sperm and testosterone factories.
Seminiferous tubules Tiny coiled tubes inside the testes where baby (immature) sperm are made.
Epididymis A long, coiled tube where sperm go to mature and are stored. Think of it as a sperm boot camp!
Vas deferens A tube that carries mature sperm from the epididymis up towards the urethra.
Seminal vesicle Adds a sugary fluid (fructose) to the sperm. This is the energy drink that powers the sperm's long journey! ⚡
Prostate gland Adds an alkaline fluid to the mix. This protects the sperm from the acidic environment inside the vagina.
Cowper's gland Releases a mucus-rich fluid that cleans out the urethra before ejaculation, protecting sperm from any leftover acidic urine.
Urethra The final exit tube for both urine and semen (but not at the same time!).
Penis The organ that delivers sperm into the female's vagina during intercourse.

The Female Reproductive System 🚶‍♀️

The female reproductive system is designed to produce eggs, secrete female hormones like estrogen, provide the perfect place for fertilization, carry a developing baby (fetus), and give birth. It includes ovaries, fallopian tubes, uterus, vagina, and external genital organs.

External Reproductive Organs
  • Labia majora: The "large lips" that enclose and protect the other external organs.
  • Labia minora: The "small lips" located just inside the labia majora. They surround the openings to the vagina and urethra.
  • Bartholin's glands: Located next to the vaginal opening, these glands produce a mucus secretion for lubrication.
  • Clitoris: A small, sensitive protrusion where the labia minora meet. It is comparable to the penis and becomes erect when stimulated.
Internal Reproductive Organs
  • Vagina: A muscular canal that connects the cervix (the lower part of the uterus) to the outside of the body. It's also known as the birth canal.
  • Uterus (womb): A hollow, pear-shaped organ that is the home for a developing baby. It can expand amazingly to hold a growing fetus! The lower part is the cervix, and the main body is the corpus.
  • Ovaries: Two small, oval-shaped glands on either side of the uterus. They produce eggs (ova) and female hormones.
  • Fallopian tubes (oviducts): Narrow tubes that serve as pathways for the egg to travel from the ovary to the uterus. This is usually where the magic happens—fertilization!
Structure Function Explained Simply ✨
Ovaries Egg factories! They produce eggs and the hormones estrogen and progesterone.
Fallopian tubes (oviducts) The pathway where the egg travels and usually meets the sperm. The site of fertilization.
Fimbria Finger-like projections at the end of the fallopian tube that sweep the egg in after ovulation.
Uterus (womb) The baby's first home! A pear-shaped organ where the embryo implants and grows. Also involved in menstruation.
Cervix The "door" to the uterus. It holds the fetus in place during pregnancy and opens (dilates) during childbirth.
Vagina Connects the uterus to the outside. It receives sperm, serves as the birth canal, and allows menstrual flow to exit.

Gametogenesis: Making Sperm and Eggs

Gametogenesis is the amazing process of creating specialized reproductive cells called gametes (sperm and eggs). It involves a special type of cell division called meiosis, where a diploid cell (with a full set of 46 chromosomes, 2n) divides to form haploid cells (with half the set, 23 chromosomes, n).

  • In males, this process is called spermatogenesis and it produces sperm.
  • In females, this process is called oogenesis and it produces eggs (ova).

Spermatogenesis 🚀

This is the journey of making sperm, and it happens inside the seminiferous tubules of the testes. It starts at puberty and continues for most of a man's life. Here’s a quick rundown:

  1. It starts with a diploid germ cell called a spermatogonium (46 chromosomes).
  2. This cell divides and grows into a primary spermatocyte (still 46 chromosomes).
  3. The first meiotic division happens, creating two secondary spermatocytes, which are now haploid (23 chromosomes each).
  4. The second meiotic division occurs, resulting in four equal-sized, haploid spermatids (23 chromosomes each).
  5. These spermatids then go through a transformation process (spermiogenesis) to become mature spermatozoa (sperm cells), complete with a head, midpiece, and tail!

These mature sperm then travel to the epididymis to finish developing and be stored.

Oogenesis 🥚

Oogenesis, the creation of an egg, is quite different. A female is born with all the primary oocytes she will ever have! The process starts even before she is born.

  1. It starts with a diploid germ cell called an oogonium in the fetal ovary.
  2. This develops into a primary oocyte (46 chromosomes), which then gets "paused" in the middle of the first meiotic division.
  3. Starting at puberty, each month, one primary oocyte finishes its first meiotic division. This division is unequal, creating one large secondary oocyte (haploid, 23 chromosomes) and a tiny polar body that disintegrates.
  4. The secondary oocyte is released during ovulation and gets "paused" again in the second meiotic division.
  5. It only completes this final division if it is fertilized by a sperm. This final division produces the mature ovum and another small polar body.

Key Difference: Spermatogenesis vs. Oogenesis

Think of it like this: Spermatogenesis is like a factory that continuously produces millions of tiny sperm from puberty onwards. Oogenesis is like having a limited collection of special eggs, where only one is prepared and released each month, and the production process is paused at different stages.

Phases of the Menstrual Cycle

The menstrual cycle is a monthly series of changes a woman's body goes through in preparation for the possibility of pregnancy. On average, it lasts about 28 days, but it can range from 20 to 45 days. It's controlled by hormones.

The cycle involves two main events:

  1. The release of a single egg from an ovary (ovulation).
  2. The preparation of the uterine lining (endometrium) to receive a fertilized egg.

If the egg isn't fertilized, the uterine lining is shed, which results in menstruation (the period).

The Four Phases of the Cycle:

  • The Follicular Phase: This is the "egg-growing" phase. The pituitary gland releases Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH), which tells the ovaries to prepare an egg inside a little sac called a follicle. The growing follicle produces estrogen.
  • The Ovulation Phase: This is the main event! Around days 12-14, a surge in estrogen causes the pituitary gland to release a lot of Luteinizing Hormone (LH). This LH surge triggers the follicle to rupture and release the mature egg. This is ovulation.
  • The Luteal Phase: After releasing the egg, the leftover follicle transforms into something called the corpus luteum. It produces high levels of progesterone and some estrogen. These hormones make the uterine lining thick and spongy, creating a perfect cozy bed for a fertilized egg.
  • The Menstruation Phase: If the egg isn't fertilized, the corpus luteum degenerates after about two weeks. Progesterone and estrogen levels drop dramatically. Without these hormones, the uterine lining breaks down and is shed from the body. This is the menstrual period.
Phase Days (approx.) What's Happening?
Menstrual Phase 1-4 The period happens; uterine lining is shed.
Follicular Phase 5-13 An egg follicle matures in the ovary, and the uterine lining starts to rebuild.
Ovulation Phase 14 The ovary releases the mature egg. 🎊
Luteal Phase 15-28 The corpus luteum forms, and the uterus prepares for a possible pregnancy.

Hormonal Feedbacks 🌡️

The menstrual cycle is regulated by a feedback system to maintain hormonal balance (homeostasis). Hormones are chemical messengers, and two key ones are Luteinizing Hormone (LH) and Follicle Stimulating Hormone (FSH), both released by the pituitary gland under the control of the hypothalamus (via GnRH).

  • Luteinizing Hormone (LH): In men, it helps produce testosterone for sperm production. In women, a mid-cycle surge of LH causes ovulation (the release of an egg). After ovulation, the remnants of the follicle form a corpus luteum, which produces progesterone.
  • Follicle Stimulating Hormone (FSH): At the start of the cycle, FSH stimulates the ovaries to grow follicles (which contain eggs). The rise in FSH leads to follicular growth and ovulation.

    • The menstrual cycle is controlled by feedback loops, similar to how a thermostat controls the temperature in a room.

    • Positive Feedback: Just before ovulation (days 12-14), high levels of estrogen from the growing follicle send a "GO!" signal to the brain, causing the massive LH surge that triggers ovulation. More estrogen leads to more LH!
    • Negative Feedback: For most of the cycle, high levels of progesterone (and estrogen) from the corpus luteum send a "STOP!" signal to the brain, preventing the release of more FSH and LH. This ensures only one egg is released per cycle. When these hormone levels drop, the "STOP!" signal is removed, and a new cycle begins with FSH rising again.

    Fertilization and Pregnancy

    1. Fertilization

    Fertilization is the union of a sperm cell and an egg cell. It typically happens in the fallopian tube. Out of millions of sperm, only one will successfully penetrate the egg. When the 23 chromosomes from the sperm fuse with the 23 chromosomes from the egg, they form a single cell with 46 chromosomes called a zygote. This is the very first cell of a new individual!

    The zygote then begins to divide rapidly as it travels down the fallopian tube to the uterus. About six days later, it attaches to the uterine wall in a process called implantation.

    2. Pregnancy

    Pregnancy, or the gestation period, is the time during which a fetus develops inside the womb. It lasts about 40 weeks (280 days) from the last menstrual period. Some common early signs of pregnancy include a missed period, tender breasts, and morning sickness.

    Mechanism of Action of Contraceptives

    Contraception refers to methods used to intentionally prevent pregnancy. People use contraception to plan their families, reduce health risks, and prevent sexually transmitted infections.

    1. Barrier Methods

    These methods create a physical barrier to stop sperm from reaching the egg. They are like putting up a roadblock. 🚧

    • Condoms (male and female): These are the only methods that also protect against most STIs!
    • Diaphragm & Cervical Cap: Soft silicone domes placed inside the vagina to cover the cervix.

    2. Non-Barrier Methods

    These methods work in other ways, often using hormones or chemicals.

    • Spermicides: Foams, creams, or gels that kill sperm. Often used with barrier methods for extra protection.
    • Oral Contraceptive Pills ("The Pill"): Contain hormones (estrogen and progesterone) that prevent ovulation. They also thicken cervical mucus, making it harder for sperm to get through.
    • Intrauterine Device (IUD): A small, T-shaped device placed in the uterus by a doctor. The copper IUD creates an inflammatory reaction that is toxic to sperm. The hormonal IUD releases progesterone to prevent pregnancy.
    • Contraceptive Implant: A tiny rod placed under the skin of the upper arm that slowly releases progesterone.
    • Contraceptive Injection: A shot of progesterone given every few months.
    • Contraceptive Ring: A flexible ring placed in the vagina that releases hormones.
    • Emergency Contraception ("Post Pill"): A high-dose hormone pill that can prevent pregnancy if taken within 72 hours of unprotected sex.
    • Sterilization: A permanent method. For women, it's tubal ligation (getting tubes tied). For men, it's a vasectomy (cutting the vas deferens).
    Method How it Works Advantage Disadvantage
    Oral Contraceptives (Pill) Inhibit ovulation with hormones Highly effective, regulates cycle Minor side effects; not for smokers over 35
    Injectables (Depo-Provera) Inhibit ovulation Effective and long-lasting Irregular bleeding; fertility may take time to return
    IUD Prevents implantation/sperm function Continuous protection for years Can cause cramps or increased flow
    Condom Mechanically blocks sperm Protects against STIs and HIV! Can break or reduce sensation slightly
    Sterilization (Tubal Ligation/Vasectomy) Permanently prevents egg/sperm from meeting Most reliable method Requires surgery; is permanent

    Causes of Infertility in Humans

    Infertility is the inability to get pregnant after a year of unprotected sex. It can affect both men and women.

    Causes of Infertility in Women

    Several factors can contribute to infertility in females. These include:

    • Thyroid problems: Prevents ovulation by being overactive or underactive.
    • Pelvic surgery complications: Can cause fallopian tube damage, scarring, or cut ovaries.
    • Cervical mucus problems: Harder mucus can prevent sperm from swimming into the vagina.
    • Fibroids: Non-cancerous growths in or around the womb that affect fertility.
    • Endometriosis: Growths of the womb lining (endometrium) that can block the ovaries.
    • Pelvic inflammatory diseases: Infections of the upper female genital tract.
    • Sterilization: A choice to permanently prevent pregnancy.
    • Medicines and drugs: The use and misuse of certain substances can cause infertility.
      • Recreational drugs like marijuana, cocaine, and cannabis.
      • Prescription medicines such as those for blood pressure, anti-depressants, and anti-psychotics.
      • Misuse of drugs and alcoholic drinks.

    Causes of Infertility in Men

    • Sperm problems: Low sperm count, poor sperm movement (motility), or abnormally shaped sperm.
    • Blockages: A blockage in the tubes that carry sperm.
    • Other factors: Chronic health problems, hormonal imbalances, or dilated veins around the testicle (varicocele).
    • Risk Factors: Smoking, alcohol/drug use, being overweight, and overheating the testicles can all negatively impact sperm production.

    The Major Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs)

    Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs) are infections passed from one person to another primarily through sexual contact. They can be caused by bacteria, viruses, or parasites.

    A. Bacterial Infections (Usually Curable with Antibiotics)

    These common infections are caused by bacteria and are typically curable with medical treatment. Prevention for all includes protective sexual contact.

    Bacterial Vaginosis

    Caused by an overgrowth of natural vaginal bacteria. Symptoms include foul-smelling or fishy discharge, itching, and burning.

    Chlamydia

    A common STI caused by the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis, which spreads through sexual contact.

    Syphilis

    A life-threatening disease caused by Treponema pallidum that progresses through four distinct stages:

    • Primary: Formation of painless sores (chancers).
    • Secondary: Rash on hands and soles of feet.
    • Latent: A period with no symptoms.
    • Tertiary: Severe complications affecting the brain, heart, and nervous system.

    Gonorrhea

    A highly contagious infection caused by Neisseria gonorrhoea. Symptoms often include painful urination and abnormal discharge, but many people show no symptoms at all.

    Chancroid

    A curable but highly contagious STI caused by the bacterium Haemophilus ducreyi. It is characterized by a painful genital ulcer.

    B. Viral Infections (Not Curable, but Treatable)

    These infections are caused by viruses. While they can often be managed with treatment, many are not curable.

    A. Genital Herpes

    A sexually transmitted infection caused by the Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV). There are two types:

    • HSV-1: Primarily affects the mouth and is spread through saliva.
    • HSV-2: Affects the genital and anal areas and is transmitted through sexual intercourse.

    B. Genital Warts

    Caused by the Human Papillomavirus (HPV), a group of viruses that can affect the skin, throat, cervix, and anus. They can cause pain and itching and significantly increase the risk of cervical and throat cancer.

    C. Molluscum Contagiosum

    A contagious viral skin infection caused by a poxvirus. It is transmitted by skin-to-skin contact and causes small, round bumps to appear on the skin.

    D. Hepatitis B

    A viral infection that attacks the liver and can lead to chronic diseases like cirrhosis and liver cancer. It is preventable with a vaccine.

    • Transmission: Spreads through blood and other bodily fluids, including sexual contact and use of non-sterile needles.
    • Symptoms: Often includes yellowing of the skin (jaundice), dark urine, extreme fatigue, and abdominal pain.
    • Prevention: Vaccines, protective sexual contact, and avoiding contact with contaminated bodily fluids.

    Understanding HIV/AIDS

    HIV is a retrovirus that attacks the immune system, specifically the CD4 helper cells (T-cells), which are crucial for fighting off infections. Over time, HIV destroys so many of these cells that the body can't fight off infections and disease anymore. This final, severe stage is called Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome (AIDS).

    Stages of HIV Infection:

    1. Acute HIV Infection (2-4 weeks after infection): People may experience flu-like symptoms. The virus is multiplying rapidly. A person is highly contagious at this stage.
    2. Chronic HIV Infection (Clinical Latency): HIV is still active but reproduces at very low levels. People may not have any symptoms. This stage can last for a decade or more without treatment.
    3. AIDS (Final Stage): The immune system is severely damaged. A person's CD4 count drops below 200 cells/mm³. They become vulnerable to serious "opportunistic infections" that a healthy immune system would normally fight off.

    C. Parasitic Infections

    • Trichomoniasis: Caused by a tiny parasite. Can cause foul-smelling discharge and itching. Curable with medication.
    • Pubic Lice ("Crabs"): Tiny insects that attach to pubic hair.
    • Scabies: Caused by a mite that burrows into the skin, causing intense itching.

    Epidemiology of STIs

    Epidemiology is the study of how diseases spread. Anyone who is sexually active is at some risk of getting an STI. The risk increases with:

    • Having unprotected sex.
    • Having multiple sexual partners.
    • Misuse of alcohol or recreational drugs, which can lead to risky decisions.
    • Injecting drugs and sharing needles.

    Harmful Traditional Practices

    Harmful traditional practices are actions based on tradition that cause physical or mental harm and violate people's rights.

    • Early Marriage: Forcing underage girls to marry, which often leads to them dropping out of school and facing serious health risks from early pregnancy.
    • Gender-based Violence: Any form of unwanted sexual contact or violence.
    • Female Genital Mutilation (FGM): The partial or total removal of external female genitalia for non-medical reasons. It has no health benefits and causes severe, lifelong health problems.

    Family Planning

    Family planning is the ability for individuals and couples to decide on the desired number, spacing, and timing of their children based on their personal, financial, and career situations.

    Risks Related to the Lack of Family Planning

    Without proper planning, families face several risks to maternal health:

    • Increased Complications: Both very early and late motherhood can lead to a higher risk of health complications and death.
    • Impact on Family: When a mother suffers from illness or complications from pregnancy, the entire family's well-being can be threatened.

    Family Planning Actions

    Effective family planning involves several key actions, including:

    • Prevention of unwanted pregnancy.
    • Ensuring family safety from STIs through counseling.
    • Providing adequate resources for couples to raise children.
    • Spacing births appropriately, such as waiting at least 6 months after a miscarriage.
    • Using methods like birth control or assisted reproductive technology.

    Effects of Alcohol, Khat, and Other Drugs

    Substance use has significant negative effects on human health, affecting physical and mental well-being.

    Effects of Alcohol

    Heavy alcohol use damages many parts of the body. Key negative effects include:

    • Damage to the brain (dementia, memory loss) and liver (cirrhosis).
    • Increased risk of various cancers (oral, breast, bowel), heart disease, and stroke.
    • Association with risky sexual behavior and the transmission of HIV.

    Effects of Chewing Khat

    Khat is a plant leaf chewed as a stimulant. It contains the drug cathinone and has both immediate and long-term negative effects.

    • Immediate Term: Alertness, increased heart rate, and suppressed appetite.
    • Long Term: Increased risk of heart attack, depression, hypertension, liver cirrhosis, and oral cancer.

    Effects of Other Drugs

    Various other drugs have severe and often long-lasting health consequences.

    • Marijuana (Cannabis): Harms the brain, breathing system, heart, and muscular coordination.
    • Cocaine: An addictive stimulant that harms the cardiovascular, breathing, and nervous systems.
    • Heroin: A very addictive drug derived from morphine (from the opium poppy) with severe negative health effects.
    • Inhalants: Household chemicals that, when inhaled, can damage the brain, liver, and heart.

    Key Concepts and Takeaways ✨

    • The musculoskeletal system is made of bones, muscles, tendons, ligaments, and cartilage, all working together to provide support, protection, and movement.
    • There are three types of muscle: cardiac (heart, involuntary), skeletal (attached to bones, voluntary), and smooth (in organs, involuntary).
    • Muscle contraction happens when myosin filaments pull on actin filaments, a process powered by ATP and triggered by calcium.
    • The skeleton has two parts: the axial skeleton (core/trunk, 80 bones) and the appendicular skeleton (limbs, 126 bones), for a total of 206 bones.
    • The male reproductive system produces and delivers sperm, while the female system produces eggs, nurtures a fetus, and gives birth.
    • Gametogenesis is the process of making gametes (sperm and eggs) through meiosis. Spermatogenesis is continuous, while oogenesis is cyclical.
    • The menstrual cycle is a complex, hormone-driven monthly process that prepares the female body for a potential pregnancy.
    • Contraception provides various ways to prevent pregnancy. Condoms are unique in also preventing most STIs.
    • STIs are serious infections spread through sexual contact. Prevention and testing are key to staying healthy.
    • Substance abuse, like drinking alcohol or using drugs, seriously impairs judgment and is strongly linked to higher rates of STIs and unplanned pregnancies.
